sentences.text: --where we let the screen door down.
I'll go when she says.
Do you want something to drink, you guys?
Oh, yeah.
Oh, my god.
Let me undo something there.
It will be easier.
Yes.
We came home one night from work and walked along the port, and there we saw the ships burning in the port-- Italian, French ships burning.
And of course, in one minute, everybody talked to each other, what happened, what happened?
Pearl Harbor happened.
And then--
The 7th of December, 1941, wasn't it?
Yeah.
I think it was the 8th.
Yeah.
No, it was '41.
And after that, the Japanese came into Hongkew and occupied Shanghai.
And things got tough because we didn't get American money anymore.
And the Russian Jewish people, they had lived there since the revolution, most of them.
I read books now where, of course, some Russian Jews were there before that because they are all big traders there, you know, with silk.
And there were a lot of philanthropists there like Sasson, Kadoorie.
These names come up from India when you read books about India.
And one of them, Kadoorie, made a school for our children there.
So our children went to the Kadoorie School, and they were supposed to speak English only.
But the teachers were from Germany, and their English was probably very poor, I don't know.
The children spoke German all day long at home and among themselves.
So in the recess time or whatever, spoke German anyway.
But it helped them when we came here.
German.
German.
There were some Czechoslovakian people there and there were some Hungarians there, Polish-- a lot of Polish people.
But most of us were German.
And then the Austrians, of course, speak German.
There were a lot of Austrians there, only that speak in a dialect.
Well, we really didn't know what was coming.
We knew it wasn't good probably, war in the East, because till then there was only eastern-- war in Europe.
But of course, rapidly things got worse.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food was scarce before.
We had food rations for some stuff, sugar, coffee, which of course, we sold to pay our little rent there.
We never touched that.
Our child grew up without sugar, with black tea and nothing.
That's why when we came here, he couldn't believe it.
Who else lives in a three-bedroom apartment?
I said, nobody.
What?
He couldn't believe.
He didn't know what cocoa was.
He drank coffee when we came to San Francisco.
We had coffee then, by the end of the war.
He wanted coffee, he didn't want milk anymore.
The babies drank soybean milk there, and he refused.
We didn't like it, either.
But babies that were born there, they didn't know anything else so they were raised with soybean milk.
Well, we were just joking with each other, my sister-in-law, our friends, saying, what are you cooking today?
Glass noodles we called it-- Some of the Chinese noodles, they are transparent.
We called them glass noodles.
And I said, oh, I cooked noodles today.
Oh, you, too?
You know?
We were kidding.
I mean, we were kind of hungry.
But like I always say, we had it better than those people in the camps.
And whoever had a piece of bread left over gave it to the other, like families.
We, we-- Like my mother-in-law and father-in-law said, if we pull together, we'll all survive.
And that's really true.
They had one day a little bit more, we had one day a little bit more.
We ate together or my mother-in-law could cook.
I couldn't-- didn't know what to cook-- how to cook, anyway.
If she bought a head of cabbage and I lit that little stove, we had a meal without fat, without anything.
But she knew what to do with it, put a little salt on it maybe.
But-- or onion.
I-- I was just absolutely helpless.
I gave up.
So I leaned a lot on them, and I could.
So it-- that was really a great help to, to us, that they were older, more experienced, and had gone through the First World War.
And for us, it was really worse.
My in-laws.
OK.
And what were your days like in Hongkew you once the Japanese arrived?
Well, we actually went to work seven days a week as long as we could.
There was nothing.
We happened to work in-- nights in restaurants.
My husband played in the bars.
So after 2:00, 3:00 in the morning, his life began.
And when I was in the neighborhood, the same place, I went with him.
And then we had a little bit of free time, then we went home and slept and off to work again.
And the child was taken care of by the in-laws or by sister-in-law.
And for a while, we had a Chinese lady, lived in the same house.
She took care of him.
So then when the bombing started--
Well, it must have been in-- I forgot now.
The war was over in '45 and it must have been around '43, '44.
We-- they didn't bomb Hongkew to begin with, Hongkew wasn't bombed.
But we saw the planes, sh, and heard them flying real low.
And the Japanese had put us-- had put the Navy-- Navy yard-- we didn't know they had weapons or ammunitions there.
We had no idea.
But we saw the Japanese playing baseball.
We didn't know what it was and somebody knew that and said, oh, that's like the Americans do, they play baseball.
We saw them.
But when the planes started flying over, we knew they flew-- Americans flew to Japan.
And when they came back, we heard that, too.
But then towards the end of the war, they tried-- they bombed Shanghai, the Americans bombed Shanghai.
And like I said, we had no idea that-- why do they want-- what do they want from Hongkew, from us?
So one day, they dropped a bomb there and 30 people, Jewish people, died and a lot of Chinese.
And after that-- before that, we didn't have any problem with Chinese people, but after that we were really buddies because our people and their people all-- everyone helped each other.
And so Henry was six years old-- or what, '38, five years old.
He said, you told me the Americans are our friends.
What do-- why do they bomb Hongkew?
And I said, well, they don't know we live here.
What can you explain a five-year-old?

What I found out now is also that the Americans save in, in-- if you fight a war on so many fronts, how can you take and think about the handful of people there or the handful of people there we don't want to hurt?
They had their targets and that's what they did.
What was it like for you once the bombings began?
Well, everybody stayed home then.
We didn't leave the district anymore.
Husband and wife stayed together.
And my husband always found work in the district.
But he came rushing home that-- one day and he stopped at the place we ate.
We couldn't eat all the food there, but fruit or vegetables.
But there was a stand, I think it was called mangoes, fruit like little apricots, and we ate them.
And he-- another guy said to him, come on, come on.
It got real gray, like nighttime almost, not quite.
And he said, come on, come on, they're coming.
And he said, I want to take that home to my family.
And he brought it and it actually saved his life.
You know, the other guys were running, and they were killed.
And he stopped.
It was just luck, he didn't know.
And then the families kind of stayed together and didn't want to be separated.
Chinese people.
I lived in a Chinese house.
My in-laws, where they lived, there were more Jewish people-- not in the same house, but like I said, a lane.
The lane was like the street here with little houses together, not-- not separate houses.
No.
We heard the cuss words-- cuss words-- and we learned to understand them.
But we took a few Japanese lessons when the Japanese first came.
They were the customers in the restaurants and bars.
And my husband went out selling all kinds of stuff, material and shoes, whatever he could lay his hands on.
He went from house to house.
And he wanted to know the figures, you know, the dollars.
Shanghai dollars.
And we had a few Japanese lessons.

Toward the end of the war, we had to take Czech lessons because the Czechoslovakian consulate all of a sudden came into a little money after the war and they let us Czechoslovakian citizens come and eat with them and that was much better food.
But that was just shortly before the war was over.
But they forced us, we had to take lessons.
We didn't have too much to do, just hi and goodbye.
And then like they were selling water in the front of the lanes, boiling water, and we had our little sticks to buy boiling water.
So we give them the stick and then they offered us alive snakes to eat.
We didn't buy them.
And my husband ate from those carts on the street-- he wasn't supposed to-- and he didn't get sick.
But I wouldn't do it.
No, we had no problems.
I mean, they spoke-- Chinese people spoke what we called pidgin English.
And we we learned to understand that and our English wasn't any better.
So it's just like no problem.
No.
Only when we arrived from the mosquito bites.
And we had-- no, I had a deep hole in my arm.
And our doctors, the European doctors, didn't-- first, we had no medications, and second, they didn't know how to treat tropical stuff-- diseases.
So they sent me-- I was sent to a Russian doctor who had been living there for years.
And he put a little salve on it, and it was gone.
But I [INAUDIBLE] it for a long time and then everything got, you know, infected.
And Henry had prickle-- prickly heat from the terrible heat-- things we didn't know.
We didn't know how to-- what to do with it.
Most people had diarrhea when we first came and a very close friend of ours died of it because we had no penicillin.
We bought penicillin for him on the black market, the family.
And that was no penicillin.
That was [? water. ?] So he died.
But we had to learn how to deal with the heat.
When we first came, we drank soda pops-- not Coca-Cola, we might not have, but soda water, Seven Up, I think they had.
And that's the worst thing we could have done.
We learned how to drink hot tea like Chinese did.
And then we were fine.
We thought, we're not going to drink hot stuff.
But we learned quickly that's we have to do.
When you live there, you have to live like the natives do.
They know how to do that.
Go ahead.
Badly.
I mean, we, we stayed outside.
We didn't go stay in that little room we had.
Nobody did.
People slept outside.
We slept out-- stayed outside till 2:00, 3:00, 4:00 in the morning, and then we went maybe upstairs.
Badly.
Well, we just, just like we always did only the food wasn't there.
But that wasn't the main thing.
We just-- whatever you had did, did the job.
So not any different.
The kids went to school.
Henry was in the-- the cheder, what they called it, when he was four years old.
And education always went on.
Well, like I said, that Kadoorie had made a school for the Jewish kids.
And teachers from Europe were teaching there.
And cheder, he went to in the synagogue where my brother-in-law was a cantor.
And a lot of Russian kids were there and Jewish kids from four years on.
They learned and studied.
Well, to begin with, the Austrians and the Germans fought on the streets, the young people, the boys.
Then they entered them into marriage.
Like everywhere else, you learn to live together.
But to begin with, there was a lot of division between German Jews and Austrian Jews and Polish Jews.
But when you're all in, in misery like that, you learn pretty quick that it's ridiculous.
No.
No.
We had one radio in one of the camps.
That's where we all went to, see where the-- how the war was going.
There were little-- little things like we had a map and we took the pins, stick pins, and when the Germans went into Russia, I remember when they pulled out and pulled back.
Every day we went in there, stick our little pins, and marked the place where they are.
But that was all.
We had no radios.
No.
That was all cut off.
My-- I had a few letters and cards here that went via Siberia.
One time I got a card through the Red Cross.
The Red Cross helped that the people could get a little mail.
So one time a card came via Siberia, another time it came from South America.
From my parents.
But not very long.
Like I said, in '43, everything stopped. '42 already, '41, I don't know.
Well, I-- the first thing I did was wrote a letter.
And I got the letter back and it said, departed without leaving forwarding address.
And then we started searching, mostly through the Red Cross.
And everybody could search for one person only because demand was so big that-- so I-- we, we didn't hear anything.
We had one list from South Africa, where aunt of mine lived.
She has died now, but my cousin still lives there.
And they sent us a list where my parents were listed as survivors in Theresienstadt.
But I said to my sister right away, I don't know if we can believe it.
Let's not go overboard with happiness.
And it never materialized.
Not with me, but she lived with her family, yes.
She went, too, and that's why I was sure that my parents would come, because the two of us were there.
They should have come then.
But we couldn't tell them.
They said, we go to America, we bring your children over to America, which was-- I can't blame them for that.
But on the other hand, they just refused to see the danger.
And a lot of other people did at their age.
We were just young, we had no future.
So what kind of life is that, Jews not allowed here, Jews not allowed there, getting spit at and people-- you don't put up with that when you're 20 and 25 years old.
But when you're in your 50s and have built a life there and you're established with seven days work practically and you're successful and you have a few dollars.
My in-laws always said, if your parents would have been as poor as we were, they would have come.
They had no other way out.
But they had that little bit of money, not that they were proud over what, but they wanted to help us with it.
Well, it depends who was-- where the money people.
A lot of Russian people, not Jews.
White Russians, they called the Russians there that have came during the revolution.
They didn't want to stay under the communists.
They re-established there.
So there were a lot of Russian people.
And before the war, naturally, where the English and Americans were interned, they were customers, too.
Before the war, yeah.
And then when the war in the Pacific was, they were interned, the English and the Americans.
And some left when they could.
Then there was no clientele anymore.
But the Russians and then like I say, when the Japanese occupied, then the Japanese were.
When the war was over, then Americans came.
And was an inflation and we had to take bags with us to carry the money home.
It wasn't worth anything when you didn't spend it that same night.
Oh, sure.
Yes.
We-- like, my husband was still working nights, but not out of the district.
He was in the district there.
And he came upstairs and he said, get dressed.
It's 4:00, 5:00 in the morning.
Everybody's dancing on the streets and the people bring out food, whatever they have, and everybody's eating and laughing and dancing.
You've got to be there.
So we went.
And it was true that they were dancing.
But the next morning, Japanese came with the guns and patrolled the streets and said, it's a lie, it's not true.
Get back.
Get back in the homes.
And we were scared.
Some people came from out of Hongkew, doesn't have to be Jewish refugee, it could have been like Russians or something.
And they heard it in the Swiss consulate, [DRINKS] on the radio.
And so it lasted for two days that they denied that the war's over and chased us back into our homes.
And we didn't go on the streets, we were scared what they'd do.
And then they had to give in.
And the police department, everything was-- nobody was in there.
So the Jewish young people, they went in-- they were-- felt so good to be the bosses for about a few days.
Then the Chinese people, they organized and they got back in.
In the war, after the war ended.
Yeah, in the Pacific, sure.
Oh, that other war-- in Europe, we heard that on that one radio and there were thousands of people around that one radio.
And there when we heard about the killing of 6 million Jews.
And I said, no, not my parents, no.
They knew so many gentiles.
They helped them and my father helped them and they worked for us.
They were all friends, all neighbors.
Everybody were friends.
They have helped my parents somehow.
My father knew, you know.
I don't know.
I thought my father can do everything.
And I said, no, it's impossible.
It can't be true.
Until we learned it was true.
That was the first time we heard about it.
And then we still didn't get papers or anything till August.
That was in May of '45 and in August the war was over in the Pacific.
We still didn't hear anything.
Then the American ships came in, and they were the customers and then they came in to Hongkew.
And I said to my son, who was five or seven years old, he said, how do Americans look?
I said, just like we.
First one he saw was a black person.
[LAUGHS] He said, we don't look like that.
So I couldn't explain too much then.
I didn't know myself.
I mean, I knew there were black people here, but I didn't know like I know now.
To explain further, I said, well, there must be some black people in America.
They're just like us, they just have a different skin color.
And so then the children-- one older boy who's in Seattle now.
He's 61, 62 years old.
He took mine-- he was about nine years old-- they skipped school.
My child had never skipped school.
The teacher came and said, your son skipped school today.
I said, it's impossible.
He wasn't there.
You better check where he is.
So those kids walked down to the port where the ships were, the American ships, and the Chinese people took them on a little boat over to the American ships.
And they dined and wined them and made them sick with all their chocolate and ice cream.
They meant well.
And the chewing gum.
Then they came back.
This boy, I still tell him that, he's 61 years old now.
He was nine years old.
He brought back a whole crate of oranges the Americans had thrown out, wanted to throw out.
So he took it, brought it back in a rickshaw.
And I said, do you have any money?
How did the rickshaw man brought you back?
I split with him.
I give him the bad ones, [LAUGHS] and I kept the good ones.
[LAUGHTER]
So it was happy for us.
But six, seven years old and he's on a Chinese little boat there, sampan, I think they called them, or junk, junk-- yeah.
I forgot.
And, and go to this big boat.
They were just absolutely beyond themselves.
Once the war had ended in the Pacific, what were your plans?
Well, I myself, I said, I don't write to that cousin for papers because I was so mad at him that he, he was really at fault that my parents didn't survive.
In America, in New York.
If he would have sent in the-- his income tax return to show that he can support the two people, my parents would have been in America.
But my sister said, well, we can't be proud now.
We, we have to get out of here.
So she wrote and then he sent the papers.
And when I came to San Francisco on the boat, was a telegram from him, welcome in this wonderful country.
Stay out west.
Henry.
I thought, he didn't have to worry that we wanted him to support us.
But he meant to us.
Another uncle was a lot nicer, why we should stay out west.
Otherwise, he would want us to come to Pittsburgh.
But he said for young people in '45-- no, '47-- it's so crowded in the east, and the west is wide open.
And you better stay out west.
What was it, we came in July '39 and we left '47 in March.
So eight years.
Well, that we had more food, and we could afford some coffee and we could afford-- only we could still not-- you couldn't eat raw vegetables, no salad or anything like that.
We had to boil everything.
But that didn't matter.
When we came here, we grabbed raw strawberries.
We hadn't eaten them.
You know, we had known everything, but the children didn't.
So we lived that in Germany.
We, we had eaten raw food, raw vegetables, raw salad and all that.
So we get that back when we came to America, but that didn't matter really.
We were just glad to have some food again.
And my mother-in-law said, my child, now I can-- we have a chicken in the pot, I can show you how to cook if you want to-- only if you want to.
I said, yeah, now I want to since she had something to show me.
During the war years, there was nothing there to cook with.
Yeah.
No, we could go wherever we wanted to.
And the Americans were the customers.
And only as a woman you had to be careful.
They were like any other men.
I said, my husband is playing in the band.
Who cares?
You know.
They weren't any better than any other.
They were in the war and they came-- celebrated.
And you had to watch out a little bit, too.
I didn't walk alone, I didn't go without anybody, I mean all by myself.
I wouldn't walk the streets.
So-- and I was young, too.
It wasn't today.
So-- but it was no comparison.
We could buy stuff.
We bought bicycles so we bicycled to work.
And--
In some restaurants in the-- in the better places, out of Hongkew.
No.
No.
I really don't.
A lot of them were owned by Jewish refugees.
They had closed maybe during this time, but then they opened again.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[DRINKS] My sister wrote to this cousin and without a letter, without a word, he sent the papers to the consulate.
And he said, don't tell my-- don't show my family my finances.
He had learned.
At least he had sent them.
We didn't care to see his finances.
We didn't want to sponge on him or not work and make him help us.
So we just needed the papers.
So the papers were there.
And then it took til '47.
You had to wait your turn, you know.
There were thousands of people going.
And finally in March-- 20th of March, '47, we left.
We will pause now for a break.
